tree.title: What should I read?
tree.description: A decision tree to help you find your next book.

branch.content: Fiction or non-fiction?
What kind of fiction?
Contemporary or classic?
Do you want to be challenged or just absorbed?
Pick something that won a major prize in the last few years and has divided readers. The arguments around it are often half the experience.
A contemporary novel with a propulsive story and real emotional depth. The kind you read in two days without noticing time passing.
European or American tradition?
Tolstoy, Flaubert, Kafka, Calvino, Pessoa. Pick the one you have been meaning to get to for years and just start it.
Steinbeck, Fitzgerald, Morrison, Roth, McCarthy. The American novel has a distinct tradition worth exploring on its own terms.
Which genre?
Cosy or dark?
Agatha Christie or a modern cosy mystery. Puzzle-forward, low stakes, satisfying resolution. Good for reading in small pieces.
Scandinavian noir or American hard-boiled. Bleak, precise, and often unexpectedly moving. Nesbo, Indridason, Lehane.
World-building or ideas first?
Find a fantasy series where the first book stands alone and at least two more exist. Do not start something unfinished.
Science fiction that uses its premise to examine something real. Le Guin, Dick, Gibson, Atwood, Butler.
Psychological dread or visceral?
Shirley Jackson, Paul Tremblay, Carmen Maria Machado. The dread is built from implication, not description. Often lingers for days.
Stephen King at his most committed, or Joe Hill. The pacing is relentless and you will not put it down.
Linked collection or standalone anthology?
Reads like a novel but lets you stop. Alice Munro, Denis Johnson, George Saunders. Works well if your reading time is fragmented.
Pick a themed or genre anthology and read one story a night. Low commitment, consistently surprising.
What area?
Broad sweep or one specific thing?
A long-form history of a civilisation, idea, or century. Peter Frankopan, Simon Schama, Yuval Noah Harari. Good for long commutes or slow weekends.
A deeply researched account of one event or crisis. When done well, it reads like a thriller. Erik Larson is the master of this format.
Accessible or technical?
Popular science at its best explains huge ideas through story. Pick something on a topic you think you are not interested in. It will change that.
If you have the background, go to the source material. University press books or the original papers on the topic.
Famous subject or personal narrative?
A biography of someone whose work you admire. Often reveals a significant gap between the public life and the private one.
A memoir about an experience very different from yours. The best ones let you live inside another life entirely.
Starting out or already familiar?
A book that introduces complex ideas through accessible writing. Pick the domain you are least confident in.
Go to the primary texts. Plato, Nietzsche, Rawls, Wittgenstein. Hard going but more rewarding than any summary of them.
Specific skill or general mindset?
Find the most technically rigorous book on the exact thing you want to improve. Not a bestseller — a textbook or practitioner guide.
Be honest with yourself about whether you have read similar books and actually changed anything. If yes, proceed. If not, try the specific skill instead.
